coredata.dc:title: Cu-CDs/H2O2 system with peroxidase-like activities at neutral pH for the co-catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine and inhibition of catalytic activity by Cr(III)
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dezhi
Li, Qiulan
Tammina, Sai Kumar
Gao, Zhe
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description:
The construction of highly efficient nanozyme activities in a simple means is vital for the wide biological and chemical applications. Present work describes a Cu-based carbon dots (Cu-CDs) with oxidase- and peroxidase-like activity for the catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) at neutral pH. The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs was finely tuned and enhanced by changing the ascorbic acid (AA) content and reduction temperature to increase the Cu+ content. The oxidation of OPD was significantly accelerated by the addition of Fe2+ ions (i.e., Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system) and presented a smaller K m (Michaelis constant) and a higher V max (maximum rate) than horseradish peroxidase (HRP) at the same mass concentration. The Cu-CDs with oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities could catalyze to produce the superoxide radical (
· O
2 -
) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) in the system. Interestingly, the catalytic oxidation of OPD to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) could be inhibited by Cr(III) ions in Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system. Therefore, a new fluorescent detection strategy was developed for the detection of Cr(III) with the linear range of 0.2−100 μM, and the detection limit of 36 nM (S/N = 3). This work provides new insight into the mechanism and kinetics of low-valence copper–carbon dots peroxidase-like and Fenton co-catalyst system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr(III)
Cu-based carbon dots
Fenton reaction
o-phenylenediamine
peroxidase-like activity
inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of unsaturated Cu-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs)
Measurement of peroxidase activity of Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system
Kinetic analysis of Cu-CDs peroxidase-like activity
Mechanism of Cu-CDs catalytic activity and fluorescent detection
Cu-CDs catalytic activity performance for the OPD
Inhibition of Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system by Cr(III) ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kunming University of Science and Technology
Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of reductive temperature (a) and AA content (b) on peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs, and high resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p (c) and Cu LMM (d).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of 0.4Cu-CDs (a). The inset shows the FFT image of 0.4Cu-CDs; X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of different Cu-CDs (b); plots of initial rate versus concentration of OPD for the catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP using different Fenton systems (c); steady-state kinetics using the Michaelis–Menten assay for OPD as a substrate (d).
Influence factors of pH (a), Cu-CDs concentration (b), Fe2+ concentration (c) and H2O2 concentration (d).
Effect of visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) on the oxidation of OPD by Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system.
Schematic illustration of radical generation by different Cu-CDs catalysts (a) and the fluorescent detection of Cr(III) by Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system (b).

The peroxidase-like performance of different catalytic systems (a); effects of hydroxyl scavenger (TBA), electron scavenger (IPA), superoxide scavenger (BQ), and hole scavenger (CH3OH) on the activity of Cu-CDs based system (b); fluorescence spectra of hydroxybenzoic acid generated under different conditions (c); different interferences for the detection of Cr(III) (d).
Comparison of the Michaelis–Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max) for OPD as substrate.
Comparison of different nanomaterials for fluorometric sensing of Cr(III).

head.ce:title: Cu-CDs/H2O2 system with peroxidase-like activities at neutral pH for the co-catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine and inhibition of catalytic activity by Cr(III)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The construction of highly efficient nanozyme activities in a simple means is vital for the wide biological and chemical applications. Present work describes a Cu-based carbon dots (Cu-CDs) with oxidase- and peroxidase-like activity for the catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) at neutral pH. The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs was finely tuned and enhanced by changing the ascorbic acid (AA) content and reduction temperature to increase the Cu+ content. The oxidation of OPD was significantly accelerated by the addition of Fe2+ ions (i.e., Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system) and presented a smaller K m (Michaelis constant) and a higher V max (maximum rate) than horseradish peroxidase (HRP) at the same mass concentration. The Cu-CDs with oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities could catalyze to produce the superoxide radical (
· O
2 -
) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) in the system. Interestingly, the catalytic oxidation of OPD to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) could be inhibited by Cr(III) ions in Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system. Therefore, a new fluorescent detection strategy was developed for the detection of Cr(III) with the linear range of 0.2−100 μM, and the detection limit of 36 nM (S/N = 3). This work provides new insight into the mechanism and kinetics of low-valence copper–carbon dots peroxidase-like and Fenton co-catalyst system.

ce:section.ce:para: The development and exploration of artificial synthetic mimicking with the catalytic function of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) have attracted widespread attention [1]. Also, for the colorimetric analysis, HRP/H2O2 plays a significant role in the catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and other relevant molecules [2,3]. In particular, colorless OPD as the peroxidase substrate for peroxidase mimics has attracted much attention due to its ability to form the yellow product (2,3-diaminophenazine, DAP) and strong fluorescence emission at 421 nm excitation wavelength [4]. Due to the high cost of preparation and the strict storage conditions, natural enzymes are not suitable for wide application. In this regard, much effort have been made for the exploration of HRP-mimicking catalysts [5,6]. Many successful enzyme-like nanomaterials have been synthesized, such as nanosheets [7,8], graphene oxide [9], nanoclusters [10,11], and nanocomposites [12]. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interest owing to its low toxicity, biocompatibility, and unique physical and chemical properties [13]. Due to its good advantages in environment and biomedicine, heteroatom-doped CDs were reported to act as catalysts mimicking the native enzyme for sensing of hydrogen peroxide, dopamine, and other target biomolecules [14]. There are many studies based on heteroatom-doped CDs as enzyme mimics [15,16]. Guo et al. explored ferrous phosphate-CDs as peroxidase mimetics for cancer cell targeted-detection [17].

To improve the enzyme activity of the carbon dots (CDs), homogeneous transition metal-doped CDs and particularly copper-doped CDs have received tremendous interest owing to its unique electronic and optical properties. Metal element-doped nanocatalysts such as Fe [5,18], Cu [19], or Au [20], were reported to prepare the peroxidase mimics. The Fe2+ and Fe3+ were often used directly in conventional Fenton reaction [7]. The Cu element doping, cheaper and simpler than Au, was proved to effectively enhance the photochemical performance of CDs (e.g. electron transfer capability and chemical reactivity) [6,21]. In this situation, Zhang et al. constructed Cu-modified CDs to enhance both electron donating and accepting abilities [21]. Also, previous work found that copper-functionalized nanoparticles (Cu2+-g-C3N4, CuS-BSA, Cu@SiO2, etc.) were served as co-catalysts for the H2O2 decomposition in Fenton-like reactions [6,19,22,23]. In general, the generated ·OH is mainly responsible for the catalytic activity [24,25], such as strong oxidation performance [26]. Since metals have the reductive property, the ·OH generation can be reduced in the Fenton system through the consumption of ·OH [26]. Unsaturated copper (Cu° and Cu+) is expected to show a reductive property to consume the ·OH in the Fenton reaction. Provided that, unsaturated metal doping sites in carbon dots provide new pathways for enzyme-like nanomaterials, which cannot be neglected in the development of enzyme mimics.

Herein, through the facile one-step pyrolysis, Cu+-doped CDs were prepared from the mixture of AA and Na2[Cu(EDTA)] under various temperature and proportions. Cu-CDs/H2O2 system displayed peroxidase-mimic for the catalytic oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2. Monovalent Cu+ significantly promoted the activity of the Cu-CDs/H2O2 system. Additionally, Fe2+ can serve as an excellent co-catalyst to accelerate the oxidation of OPD in Cu-CDs/H2O2 system. Moreover, the radicals generated from Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system were studied, and the synergistic effects of Fe2+ and Cu(I)-CDs are also investigated. Interestingly, the Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system exhibits reduced production of DAP in the presence of Cr(III). The inhibitory effect of Cr(III) on the Fenton-like system was developed as a fluorescent nanosensor toward Cr(III), which is different from Fenton strategy in the literature. This work provides new insight into Fenton co-catalyst system.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid copper (II) disodium salt (Na2[Cu(EDTA)], 98.0 %) was purchased from the TCI Shanghai Co., Ltd. (China). o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were purchased from the Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), ascorbic acid (AA, 99.7 %) and hydrogen peroxide (30 wt.%) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents used were of analytical grade. Distilled water was provided by a Millipore-Q Academic purification system (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of unsaturated Cu-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu-CDs were synthesized by carrying out the thermolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and AA in different molar ratios [27]. The melting temperature of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and AA are 252 ℃ and 190–192 ℃, respectively. In brief, Na2[Cu(EDTA)] (1.0 g) was mixed with a certain amount of AA. Then the mixture was transferred to a corundum boat in a quartz glass tube and heated at 250–400 ℃ for 120 min. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown powder was washed with acetone for several times. Then the powder was dissolved in 20 mL distilled water, after which the supernatant was separated by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 20 min). The solid powder of Cu-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying the supernatant, and later CDs were dispersed in distilled water (2 mg mL−1) and stored at 4 ℃ for later use. The different Cu-CDs obtained by adding appropriate AA (corresponding to 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 g AA) were defined as 0.2Cu-CDs, 0.4Cu-CDs, 0.6Cu-CDs and 0.8Cu-CDs, respectively. To further understand the roles of AA and Cu+ on the peroxidase activity, the Cu-CDs were synthesized similar to the process mentioned above without the addition of AA, and named it as 0Cu-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of peroxidase activity of Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs was evaluated via the catalytic oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2. A total of 100 μL of Cu-CDs and 100 μL of Fe2+ (2 mM) were mixed into 100 μL of OPD solution (5 mM) and the solution was diluted to 4 mL. Before the reaction, the initial pH was adjusted to 7.0 with H2SO4 (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). Subsequently, 100 μL H2O2 (25 mM) was added into the mixture. The fluorescence of Cu-CDs is weak, and it will not interfere with the fluorescence of DAP. So after the incubation at 37 ℃ for 25 min, the fluorescence spectrum signals of DAP were detected at 421 nm excitation wavelength immediately.

The effects of Fe2+ concentration (0−75 μM), incubation time (0–45 min), temperature (27–57 °C), pH (3.0–10.0), H2O2 concentration (0−0.938 μM), Cu-CDs synthesis temperature (250−400 °C) and concentration (0–75 μg mL−1) were assessed to determine their influence on the peroxidase-like activity.

The inhibition of Cr(III) ions was conducted by adding the various concentration of Cr3+ to the mixture of Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) + Fe2+ (50 μM) + OPD (125 μM). The volume was diluted to 4 mL and pH was adjusted to 7.0 with H2SO4 (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). After incubated at 37 ℃ for 25 min, the fluorescence emission intensity were measured at 421 nm excitation wavelength. Likewise, the same experiment was carried out in the absence of Fe2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of Cu-CDs peroxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The Michaelis–Menten constant (K m) can be used to assess the affinity of the enzyme for its substrate. The kinetic evaluation was conducted by changing the concentration of one substrate (OPD) at fixing the other substrate. Experiments were carried out by mixing the different concentrations of OPD (0.125–1.25 mM) with (a) 0Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM), (b) 0.4Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) and (c) 0.4Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) + Fe2+ (50 μM) at pH 7.0 (37 °C). Later, the samples were taken out at regular time intervals and the concentration of DAP were measured by UV–vis at 415 nm according to the Beer-lambert law (see Supplementary data). A Lineweaver–Burk double reciprocal was used to calculate the kinetic parameters:
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V
m a x
(
1
[ S ]
) +
1
V
m a x
Where V and V max refer to the initial and maximal reaction velocity, where [S] represents the concentration of substrate, and K m relates the Michaelis constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Cu-CDs catalytic activity and fluorescent detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the generation of radicals, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) and isopropanol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), and CH3OH were employed as the quenching agents of hydroxyl radicals, electrons, superoxide radicals, and holes, respectively [26]. In brief, TBA, IPA, BQ and CH3OH was treated with (a) Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + OPD (125 μM) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) and (b) Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + OPD (125 μM) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) + Fe2+ (50 μM), respectively. In addition, benzoic acid (BA) was used as the probe to detect the production of ·OH in system. BA can react with ·OH to generate hydroxybenzoic acid. The details are as follows: BA were mixed with the Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (0.625 mM), Fe2+ (50 μM), Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM), Fe2+ (50 μM) + H2O2 (0.625 mM), Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) + Fe2+ (50 μM), respectively. And then the photoluminescence signal of hydroxybenzoic acid were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

In this work, control experiments were performed to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(III) detection. Herein, Cr(III) ions were (a) reacted with the Cu-CD-catalyzed products (DAP) and (b) directly mixed with the Cu-CDs (50 μg mL−1) + OPD (125 μM) + H2O2 (0.625 mM) + Fe2+ (50 μM) solution to investigate their influence on fluorescence, respectively.

The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs synthesized at different temperatures and proportions was detected by measurement of fluorescence of the yellow product (DAP), which was frequently used as a peroxidase substrate in the colorimetric analysis [28]. The catalytic efficiency of Cu-CDs catalysts with different reduction temperatures were illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The Cu-CDs synthesized at 400 °C had displayed the lowest fluorescence signal (169 a.u.) and also the color of the powder was changed from dark brown at 270 °C to dark at 400 °C (inset picture in Fig. 1a and Fig. S1), due to the excessive carbonization of CDs. Fig. S2 shows the XRD spectrum of the precipitate after centrifugation of the mixture containing 0.4Cu-CDs. The results showed that the peaks attributed to graphite carbon (26.4°) disappeared when the Cu-CDs was synthesized at 400 °C [29]. The higher reduction temperature caused the increase of Cu° content at 43.1° and 50.3° [29]. However, the highest peroxidase-like activity was obtained at 270 °C (353 a.u.). The further increase in synthesis temperature is not beneficial for the rise of Cu+ content (Fig. 1c and d) [22]. This result indicated that the reduction temperature can significantly affect the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs.

Since the reduction of Cu has a significant effect on peroxidase-like activity, appropriate AA was mixed with Na2[Cu(EDTA)] to enhance the reduction of Cu during the synthesis process. Under the optimum temperature (270 °C), the fluorescence signal of DAP was increased with increasing the AA content and reached a maximum (518 a.u.) at 0.4 g of AA (Fig. 1b). Further increasing of AA content might lead to further the conversion of Cu+ to Cu°, which is not beneficial to the ·OH generation [22].

To confirm the morphologies and formation of Cu-CDs catalysts, TEM and FFT image (Fig. 2a), XPS patterns (Fig. 2b) and FT-IR spectra (Fig. S3) of Cu-CDs were obtained. As shown in Fig. S3, the peaks at 1010 cm−1 was assigned to Cu-N group, indicating the formation of Cu coordination complexes [30]. Fig. 1c and d displays the Cu 2p and Auger Cu LMM signals of 0Cu-CDs, 0.4Cu-CDs and 0.8Cu-CDs. The deconvolution of the asymmetrical of Cu 2p signals was attributed to the different Cu species. The presence of Cu 2p peaks at 935.5 eV and 950.1 eV revealed the existence of Cu2+. The Cu 2p peaks appeared at 935.5 eV and 950.1 eV demonstrated the reduced Cu species [31,32]. The low Cu 2p peaks at 935.5 eV and 950.1 eV for 0.4Cu-CDs demonstrated the reduction of Cu2+. Two peaks at 563.2 eV and 572.3 eV confirmed the presence of Cu+ and Cu° [27]. Thus, proper AA content is beneficial for the production of Cu+ and improves the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs. Subsequently, 0.4Cu-CDs was chosen for the following reaction system.

The kinetic assay of Cu-CDs was conducted by measuring the initial rate of OPD at different concentrations. Fig. 2c and d depicts the typical Michaelis–Menten curves for OPD. K m indicates the affinity of the catalyst for the substrate, which can be obtained from the x-intercept. Furthermore, a lower K m value indicates a higher affinity of Cu-CDs for the OPD. Also, V max is an indicator of the catalytic activity, which can be obtained from the y-intercept.

The Michaelis–Menten constant (K m) and the maximum rate of reaction (V max) values for Cu-CDs nanocatalysts are given in Table 1 . The peroxidase-like activity of OPD by Cu-CDs follows the following order: 0.4Cu-CDs + Fe2+ > 0.4Cu-CDs > 0Cu-CDs > HRP. The K m values of 0.4Cu-CDs + Fe2+ and 0.4Cu-CDs with OPD are lower than that of the HRP enzyme [33], which suggest a higher binding affinity of Cu-CDs to OPD. Furthermore, the addition of Fe2+ in oxidation processes has a positive effect at neutral pH. Fe2+ palys a key role in the decomposition of H2O2, which is conducive to the OH generation [34]. Therefore, V max value increases significantly with the addition of Fe2+.

For the oxidation of OPD, the following parameters were optimized in detail (see Supplementary data): (a) solution pH, (b) temperature, (c) Cu-CDs concentration, (d) Fe2+ concentration, (e) H2O2 concentration and (f) incubation time. The results were shown in Fig. 3 , Figs. S4 and S5. At neutral pH, the optimal co-catalytic activity was obtained, which is different from the conventional Fenton reaction with an optimal pH of 3.8 [22]. The maximum intensity was obtained when Fe2+ and H2O2 were 50 μM and 0.625 mM, respectively. Beyond this level, the fluorescence intensity began to descend. The reason may be due to the removal of ·OH radicals by excessive Fe2+ and H2O2 [34]. The following experimental conditions were found to give best results: (a) pH 7.0 of solution, (b) incubation temperature of 37 °C, (c) 50 μg mL−1 of Cu-CDs, (d) 50 μM of Fe2+, (e) 0.625 mM of H2O2, and (f) incubation time of 25 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu-CDs catalytic activity performance for the OPD
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the effect of visible light on the Fenton-like reaction, the catalytic experiments were performed in the dark and visible light conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , under the optimal conditions, there was a noticeable difference in the fluorescence intensity of 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2+Fe2+ detection system under the dark and visible light conditions. This might be due to the fact that 0.4Cu-CDs can release Cu+ under visible light irradiation [27]. Sequentially, the visible light-induced release of Cu+ could form the insoluble hydroxide precipitates under the neutral conditions and hindered the peroxidase-like activity of 0.4Cu-CDs.

Fig. 5 schematically represents the oxidation of OPD by Cu-CDs catalyst. The peroxidase-like activity of different catalytic systems for the oxidation of OPD was evaluated. As seen in Fig. 6a, the poor activity of Fe2+ + H2O2 indicated that the conventional Fenton reaction has a low OPD peroxidase-like activity under neutral conditions. However, the high peroxidase-like activity was observed with 0.4Cu-CDs+ H2O2, which indicated that the 0.4Cu-CDs was directly involved in the decomposition of H2O2, which is consistent with other reports [22]. Moreover, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe2+ in the absence of H2O2 is lower than 100 a.u., so H2O2 plays an essential role in the conventional Fenton system. Moreover, the 0.4Cu-CDs could also exhibit the catalytic effect on OPD in the absence of H2O2. The intrinsic oxidase-mimic activity of Cu-CDs should be attributed to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) from O2 upon the catalytic oxidation effects of the Cu-CDs [35]. The Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system displayed a promising oxidation effect for DAP (1000 a.u.), which was much higher than that of the Fe2+/H2O2 system (455 a.u.) and Cu-CDs/H2O2 system (776 a.u.), because Fe2+ is beneficial to the degradation of H2O2. The oxidation performance of 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 plus Fe2+ system was optimal.

To explore the reasons for the promoted oxidation of OPD to DAP, the main ROS for DAP production were investigated by scavenger tests. TBA, IPA, BQ and CH3OH were used as the quenching agents of ·OH radicals, e−,
· O
2 -
radicals, h+, respectively. It is well-known that ·OH is considered to play a key role in the conventional Fenton reaction. Seen from Fig. 6b, after the addition of TBA into the OPD solution, the Cu-CDs co-catalytic activity performance got an obvious decrease from 790 a.u. to 240 a.u. (fluorescence intensity), indicating the predominant role of ·OH in DAP production. Additionally, BQ strongly inhibited the DAP production, suggesting the significant contribution of
· O
2 -
in the reactions. These results confirmed that ·OH and
· O
2 -
radicals were all produced to facilitate the DAP production in 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 and 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 plus Fe2+ system. It is different from the previous report that only ·OH was produced to facilitate the DAP production [5,35]. Fig. 6c illustrates the fluorescence signal of hydroxybenzoic acid in different system. Compared to the Fe2+/H2O2 system, 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 and 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 plus Fe2+ system displayed a higher fluorescence signal. This observation also showed that, in contrast to the experiments performed with 0.4Cu-CDs/H2O2 system, the addition of Fe2+ exhibited an obvious increase of ·OH. This suggested that the conversion efficiency of H2O2 into ·OH radical is promoted by the addition of Fe2+.

Due to its good redox effect, Cu(I)-doped CDs as the catalyzer was utilized in “click chemistry” applications [27]. Sun et al. proved that Cu+ is very helpful to the decomposition of H2O2 and the generation of ·OH, followed by Cu2+ and Cu° [22]. Similarly, it was reported that copper had the same Fenton reaction effects as iron [36]. The reduction of Cu2+ by H2O2 (Eq. 3, k 2 = 4.6 × 102 M−1 S−1) is faster than that of Fe3+ by H2O2 [Eq. 5, k 4 = (0.1–2) × 10−2 M−1 S−1] [37,38], which loosens the limited rate step. The increased content of Cu+ led to the enhanced the production of DAP, which further indicated that Cu+ ion in the system is responsible for the high activity of the Cu-CDs based system (Fig. 1d). There are two main reasons for the decrease of Cu+ content: (1) Cu+ was converted to Cu2+ through Cu-CDs based Fenton-like reaction; (2) the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ was accelerated by Cu+. Therefore, it can be inferred that the H2O2 decomposition follows the catalytic decomposition mechanism (Eqs. 2–6):
(2) Cu+ + H2O2 → Cu2+ + ·OH + −OH…k 1 = 1 × 104 M−1 S−1
(3) Cu2+ + H2O2 → Cu+ + ·O2 − + H+…k 2 = 4.6 × 102 M−1 S−1
(4) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + ·OH + −OH…k 3 = 76 M−1 S−1
(5) Fe3+ + H2O2 → Fe2+ + ·O2 − + H+…k 4 = (0.1–2) × 10−2 M−1 S−1
(6) Cu+ + Fe3+ → Fe2+ + Cu2+

The effect of the Cu-CDs on Fe3+/Fe2+ conversion was investigated further to study the reaction mechanism. The soluble Fe2+ in solution was measured with UV–vis spectroscopy by adding 1,10-phenanthroline monohydrate (Fig. S6). By adding the Cu-CDs to the above mixture, a peak observed at 511 nm assigned to Fe2+, which was owing to the acceleration of Fe3+/Fe2+ conversion in Cu-CDs + Fe3+/H2O2 system. It can be concluded that the presence of Cu+ in system is beneficial to accelerate the conversion of Fe3+/Fe2+ conversion and decomposition of H2O2 to form ·OH and
· O
2 -
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system by Cr(III) ions
ce:section.ce:para: To further highlight the potential applications of the Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system in the environment, the interference of metal ions were carried out under the optimal experimental conditions, such as Na+, K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Al3+ and Cd2+. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of DAP was almost undisturbed by most metal. Interestingly, Cr(III) ions showed the highest remarkable response, and the fluorescence intensity was hardly affected by Cr(VI) ions, which provide the possibility of Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 system for the detection of Cr(III) [39]. In order to confirm the mechanism of Cr(III) quenching, a series of experiments were conducted in the absence/presence of Cr(III). When Cr(III) was absent, the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs in the oxidation of OPD to DAP was not be affected. Whereas in the presence of Cr(III), the fluorescence intensity of the detection system decreases. Considering the fluorescence quenching of DAP by Cr(III), Cr(III) was directly added into the DAP solution. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of DAP showed a negligible change, which indicated that Cr(III) was responsible for inhibiting DAP production.

Despite the different aqueous solubility behavior of Cr(III) and Cr(VI), both Cr(III) and Cr(VI) can react with H2O2 and generate ·OH. In the conventional Fenton reaction, Cr(VI) was directly reduced to Cr(III) via a series of reactions with H2O2. At the same time, Fenton-like strategy has been applied to the reduction of heavy metal ions (like Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cr6+) and the remediation of aqueous organic pollutants (phenol) at acidic pH [26,34,40]. Both Cr(VI) and Cr(III) exhibit a pH-dependent reaction mechanism with H2O2. At neutral pH, the redox conversion [Cr(III) → Cr(VI)] is accompanied by the ·OH generation [41], and the concentration of ·OH was enhanced gradually as the reaction progresses. However, under neutral and near-alkaline conditions, the free Cr(III) was in the form of CrO2 −. The oxidation process had consumed the available H2O2 in reaction (Eq. 7), results in inhibition of the DAP production. Thereby, the inhibition of Cr(III) on the Cu-CDs + Fe2+/H2O2 reaction system provided a possibility for the Cr(III) detection.
(7) CrO2 − + H2O2 + −OH → CrO4 2− + H2O

Cr(III) is considered as an essential element for animals and human body [42]. Herein, we studied the detection of Cr(III) in the presence of Cu-CDs/H2O2 system. The fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs/H2O2 detection system was declined linearly with the increasing concentration of Cr(III) (Fig. S7). The linear range of Cr(III) ions is 0.2−100 μM, and the detection limit is 36 nM (S/N = 3). As shown in Table 2 , comparing with other reported Cr(III) detection methods, the linear range and detection limit of this method are equal or even better than that of other methods.

The practicability of the developed detection system was evaluated through detecting the Cr(III) in actual water samples including tap water, lake water and pond water (Kunming, China). After filtering with 0.2 μm nylon membrane filter and adjusting the pH to 7, the samples spiked at three concentration levels (0.5, 10, and 50 μM) were measured. The results were listed in Table S1. The recoveries of Cr(III) in actual water samples ranged from 92.8 to 103.1 %, and the relative standard deviations were all below 3.1 %. These results demonstrated the well selectivity and practicability of the Cu-CDs/H2O2 system for Cr(III) detection in real water samples.

In paper, Cu(I)-doped CDs with oxidase- and peroxidase-like activity were fabricated through thermolysis and investigated for the OPD oxidation. It was found that the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDs was significantly influenced by the reduction temperature and AA content. 0.4Cu-CDs exhibited excellent performance for the OPD oxidation, indicating that the increase of Cu(I) content in Cu-CDs is beneficial for the decomposition of H2O2. Also, the generated
· O
2 -
and ·OH in the Cu-CDs/H2O2 system were conducive to the reduction of ferric species and the oxidation of OPD. Under neutral pH conditions, the addition of Fe2+ effectively increased the activity of the Cu-CDs/H2O2 system by promoting the H2O2 decomposition. The results of Michaelis–Menten kinetics showed that the peroxidase-like activity follows the following order: 0.4Cu-CDs + Fe2+ > 0.4Cu-CDs > 0Cu-CDs > HRP. Interestingly, Cr(III) ions were used to inhibit the Cu-CDs/H2O2 + Fe2+ system to reduce the production of DAP, rather than quenching the fluorescence of DAP, which is different from other literatures. The OPD oxidation by Cu-CDs/H2O2 + Fe2+ was further exploited for the selective detection of Cr(III), with a good linear range (0.2−100 μM) and detection limit (36 nM). Based on these observations, unsaturated Cu-doped CDs showed excellent peroxidase-like activity, and provided a new perspective for the peroxidase-like and Fenton co-catalyst system.
Dezhi Yang: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Qiulan Li: Validation, Software. Sai Kumar Tammina: Investigation. Zhe Gao: Validation. Yaling Yang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (Grant number 2019P20173118001). Yang Dezhi gratefully acknowledges financial support from the 2018 and 2019 Kunming University of Science and Technology Graduate Research and International Exchange Project Fund, and Yunnan Province PhD Academic Newcomer Fund.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dezhi Yang is a PhD at Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on three primary domains: (a) fluorescent probe, (b) carbon quantum dots, and (c) environmental and food analysis (such as heavy metals and phenols).
Qiulan Li is a master student at Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. Her research examines the oxidation and degradation properties of materials.
Sai Kumar Tammina is a postdoctoral researcher at Kunming University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on the application of carbon quantum dots in analytical methods.

Zhe Gao is a master student at Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. Her research focuses on the synthesis of carbon quantum dots and its application in microbial bacteriostasis.

Yaling Yang is a Professor of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. She has an interest in analytical chemistry, such as environmental analysis and food analysis. She also has good experience in analytical chemistry.
